mnemonics.title: Types of Servers
mnemonics.content: WAFPA: Web, Application, File, Proxy, Application - helps remember main server categories

mnemonics.title: HTTP Status Codes
mnemonics.content: 200 = OK (Success), 300 = Redirect, 400 = Client Error, 500 = Server Error - Numerical pattern for remembering categories

mnemonics.title: Mail Server Ports
mnemonics.content: SMTP Sends Mail (25), POP3 Picks (110), IMAP Is More Advanced (143) - Remember receiving protocols

acronyms.usage: Advanced email retrieval allowing server-side management (port 143)
Transferring files between client and server (ports 20,21)
How client applications interact with server services

visualTips.visualization: Think of a restaurant: Client = Customer (orders food), Server = Waiter (takes order to kitchen), Kitchen = Database/Backend. Customer never goes to kitchen directly!
Think of IP address as building address and port number as apartment number. Multiple services can run on same building (IP) at different apartments (ports).
Like a library: Client = Person asking for book, Server = Librarian finding and giving book, HTTP = Request form and response slip with status.
Thick client = Full restaurant where cooking happens at your table. Thin client = Room service where everything comes from kitchen.

analogies.analogy: Restaurant analogy: Customer (client) places order, Waiter (network) takes request to Kitchen (server), Food (response) is served back. Customer never enters kitchen directly.
Like a library - one central place (server) where all books (resources) are stored. Students (clients) come to library to borrow/return books.
Like a hotel receptionist - guests (clients) make requests, receptionist forwards to appropriate department, can cache information, and provides anonymity.
Phone call: Your phone number = IP address, Extension = Port number. To connect, you need both the main number and correct extension.
